choice: (a) Deferrals come off the top: `income − deferral` is the pool the expense-absorption rule then operates on. Deferrals land in tax-deferred and are invested at the Plan's target allocation at contribution; per your 'returns reinvested in-asset' rule there is no subsequent rebalancing, so drift is allowed. (b) Reinvest the RMD overage into the *same asset withdrawn from*, preserving your buy-and-hold/no-rebalance philosophy and avoiding an injected S&P assumption; fall back to S&P 500 only when the withdrawal was pro-rata across multiple sleeves and there's no single source asset.
rationale: Your 'returns reinvested in that asset' rule already settles rebalancing policy, so the only undecided piece for deferrals is the entry weighting — target allocation at contribution is the one choice consistent with both the contribution spec and the no-rebalance rule. Taking deferrals off the top keeps them from being double-counted against the expense-absorption pool and preserves the tax-deferred/taxable split that RMDs and withdrawal order depend on. For the RMD overage, 'same asset withdrawn from' is the allocation-neutral default under buy-and-hold; S&P 500 is simpler but silently imposes an equity tilt on the new taxable account, so it's better as an explicit fallback than the primary rule.
